title: A Function in the Wrong Room
summary: A booking API returns nothing but errors after a routine deployment. The function exists, the code is correct, yet every call fails.

personality.role: quiet observer on the engineering team
personality.demeanor: states facts and lets them accumulate, does not raise their voice

facts: sales engineer just messaged, demo is in fifteen minutes
she asked if the endpoint is ready
function exists according to the pipeline, but API Gateway says it does not
these two facts do not agree
someone on the team mentioned they changed the CI environment variables last week
something about default credentials for disaster recovery testing
function is not broken -- it deployed successfully
question is where it deployed to
API Gateway is looking for it in a specific region

hints.hint: function exists but API Gateway cannot find it -- what could make a working function invisible to a service that should see it?
AWS resources are regional -- a Lambda function in one region does not exist in another, check where the API Gateway is looking
check what region the CLI is configured to use with aws configure list, look at the region setting
CI/CD pipeline has AWS_DEFAULT_REGION set to us-west-2 but API Gateway is in us-east-1 -- function was deployed to the wrong region
redeploy the function to us-east-1, or confirm it exists in us-west-2 with aws lambda get-function --function-name calendine-booking-api --region us-west-2

glossary.AWS Region: A geographic area where AWS runs its data centers. Each region is independent -- a resource created in us-east-1 does not exist in us-west-2. Think of regions as separate buildings that do not share rooms.
glossary.ARN: Amazon Resource Name. A unique address for any resource in AWS. It includes the service, region, account ID, and resource name. Example: arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:my-function.
glossary.AWS_DEFAULT_REGION: An environment variable that tells the AWS CLI which region to send commands to. If no --region flag is passed explicitly, the CLI uses this value. CI/CD pipelines often set it, and a wrong value sends deployments to the wrong place.
glossary.Lambda function: A piece of code that AWS runs on demand without you managing a server. You upload your code, and AWS executes it when triggered -- by an API call, a schedule, or another AWS service.
glossary.API Gateway: An AWS service that sits in front of your backend and accepts HTTP requests from clients. It routes those requests to a Lambda function, an EC2 instance, or another service. It lives in a specific region and expects its backend to be in that same region.
glossary.ResourceNotFoundException: An error AWS returns when you ask for a resource that does not exist in the region you are looking in. It does not mean the resource was deleted -- it may simply be in a different region.
glossary.aws configure list: A CLI command that shows the active AWS configuration -- access key, secret key, region, and where each value comes from (environment variable, config file, or instance metadata). The single most useful diagnostic command for credential and region issues.
glossary.CI/CD pipeline: An automated system that builds, tests, and deploys your code when you push changes. Common tools include GitHub Actions, CodePipeline, and Jenkins. The pipeline inherits environment variables that control where deployments go.

narrative_arc.call: alert arrives, stakes established from story.md Opening
narrative_arc.threshold: player opens CloudWatch, sees ResourceNotFoundException pointing at ARN in us-east-1, Lambda console in us-east-1 shows nothing
narrative_arc.trials: code correct, pipeline logs show success, IAM role exists -- everything looks right except function is missing from the expected region, player may check permissions or handler config before questioning geography
narrative_arc.revelation: aws configure list shows region is us-west-2 from AWS_DEFAULT_REGION, function deployed to wrong region, sitting in us-west-2 since 14:12 UTC healthy and idle
narrative_arc.return: redeploy to us-east-1, add explicit --region flags to deploy scripts, correct CI/CD environment variable, delete orphaned function in us-west-2

components.name: calendine-booking-api (API Gateway)
components.role: Accepts HTTP requests from client browsers for booking creation and calendar operations. Routes requests to a Lambda function via proxy integration.
connections: Returns 500 to clients when the Lambda function is not found
components.failure_impact: If the referenced Lambda function does not exist in us-east-1, every API call returns ResourceNotFoundException wrapped in a 500 Internal server error.

components.name: calendine-booking-api (Lambda function)
components.role: Handles booking creation and calendar sync. Reads and writes to DynamoDB table calendine-bookings.
connections: Triggered by API Gateway via proxy integration
components.failure_impact: If deployed to the wrong region, the function works correctly but is invisible to the API Gateway in us-east-1. Zero invocations despite being healthy.

components.name: GitHub Actions CI/CD pipeline
components.role: Builds and deploys the Lambda function code. Uses aws lambda update-function-code with an S3 deployment package.
connections: Deploys to whichever region AWS_DEFAULT_REGION points to
Reads deployment artifact from calendine-deploy S3 bucket
components.failure_impact: If AWS_DEFAULT_REGION is set to the wrong region and no --region flag is specified, the function deploys to a region where no other infrastructure expects it.

components.name: CloudWatch Logs
components.role: Captures API Gateway execution logs in us-east-1 and Lambda invocation logs in whichever region the function runs.
components.failure_impact: Logs exist in both regions but tell different stories. The API Gateway logs show errors; the Lambda logs in us-west-2 show successful test invocations from earlier.

system_narration.data_flow: Client browser sends POST /bookings to API Gateway in us-east-1. API Gateway invokes Lambda ARN in us-east-1. Lambda function processes booking and writes to DynamoDB. Response returns through API Gateway to client.
system_narration.what_broke: The CI/CD pipeline environment had AWS_DEFAULT_REGION=us-west-2, set during disaster recovery testing the previous week. The deploy command had no explicit --region flag. The function was deployed to us-west-2 while the API Gateway in us-east-1 referenced an ARN in us-east-1. The function did not exist where the API Gateway was looking.

resolution.root_cause: The CI/CD pipeline environment variable AWS_DEFAULT_REGION was set to us-west-2. The Lambda function calendine-booking-api was deployed to us-west-2, but the API Gateway integration in us-east-1 references the function ARN in us-east-1. The function does not exist in us-east-1, so every API call returns ResourceNotFoundException.

fix_criteria.description: Player identifies that the Lambda function was deployed to us-west-2 while the API Gateway expects it in us-east-1
Player identifies that AWS_DEFAULT_REGION in the CI/CD environment is the source of the wrong deployment region
Player proposes redeploying the function to us-east-1 or updating the CI/CD pipeline region configuration

learning_objectives: AWS resources are regional -- a Lambda function deployed in us-west-2 does not exist in us-east-1
The AWS CLI resolves region from (in order): --region flag, AWS_DEFAULT_REGION env var, AWS_REGION env var, config file -- understanding this chain is critical for debugging deployment issues
aws configure list shows the active region and its source, making it the first diagnostic command for region-related issues
API Gateway Lambda integrations reference a function ARN that includes the region -- the function must exist in that specific region
sop_steps: Check which region the CLI is currently targeting. Run aws configure list and look at the region row -- it tells you where commands are being sent and whether that setting comes from an environment variable, a config file, or a flag you typed explicitly.

Try to look up the function in the region where it should be. Run aws lambda get-function --function-name calendine-booking-api --region us-east-1. If it comes back 'not found,' the function was never deployed there.
Now check the other region. Run aws lambda get-function --function-name calendine-booking-api --region us-west-2. If it shows up here, the deploy went to the wrong place.

Redeploy the function to the correct region by adding an explicit --region flag: aws lambda update-function-code --function-name calendine-booking-api --region us-east-1 --s3-bucket calendine-deploy --s3-key booking-api/latest.zip. The --region flag overrides any default and sends the deploy exactly where you want it.

Fix the root cause in the CI/CD pipeline. The environment variable AWS_DEFAULT_REGION controls where the CLI sends commands when no --region flag is given. Update it to us-east-1 in the GitHub Actions workflow file.

Add a permanent safeguard: put an explicit --region us-east-1 flag directly in the deploy command inside the pipeline script. This way, even if the environment variable changes again, deploys still go to the right place.

Clean up the accidental copy. Delete the orphaned function sitting in the wrong region so it does not confuse anyone or cost money: aws lambda delete-function --function-name calendine-booking-api --region us-west-2.
Verify everything works end-to-end by calling the API endpoint and confirming you get a 200 response instead of the previous error.

related_failure_modes.scenario: The deployment package (zip file) is stored in S3 in a different region than the function
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: Here the function itself is in the right region, but the zip file it needs to load is stored in an S3 bucket in a different region. AWS requires the S3 bucket and the Lambda function to be in the same region. When they are not, AWS returns a PermanentRedirect error instead of deploying the code.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Create a separate S3 bucket for deployment artifacts in each region where you run Lambda functions. Have your pipeline verify that the bucket region matches the function region before deploying.

related_failure_modes.scenario: The API Gateway points to a function in a different AWS account
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: The function is in the correct region, but it lives in a different AWS account. Every AWS resource has a unique address called an ARN (Amazon Resource Name), and that address includes the account number. If the account number in the ARN does not match, AWS reports the function as 'not found' -- even though it exists somewhere. Allowing cross-account access requires extra permission configuration on the function.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Before deploying, run aws sts get-caller-identity to confirm you are operating in the correct account. Hard-code the account ID in the API Gateway integration so mismatches are caught immediately.

related_failure_modes.scenario: Two different region settings (AWS_REGION and AWS_DEFAULT_REGION) conflict with each other
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: AWS has two environment variables that control the target region: AWS_DEFAULT_REGION (used by the CLI) and AWS_REGION (preferred by some programming language SDKs). If both are set to different values, the CLI might deploy to one region while application code running in a different language SDK talks to another region.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Always set both AWS_REGION and AWS_DEFAULT_REGION to the same value in your CI/CD environment. Even better, use explicit --region flags so you never depend on either variable.

related_failure_modes.scenario: The function exists but the API Gateway references a specific version or alias that was never published
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: The function is in the right region and the right account, but the API Gateway's address for the function includes a version label (like ':prod') that does not exist yet. Think of it like mailing a letter to the right building but the wrong apartment number -- the building exists, but the specific unit does not. AWS returns the same 'not found' error.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Use version labels (called aliases in Lambda) that your deployment pipeline creates automatically. After each deploy, verify the alias exists by running aws lambda get-alias before updating the API Gateway.

sop_practices: Always spell out the region explicitly in deploy commands using the --region flag. Do not rely on environment variables or config files to pick the right region -- those defaults are the most common reason deploys end up in the wrong place.

When a deploy produces a 'resource not found' error (called ResourceNotFoundException), the first thing to check is which region the CLI is targeting. Run aws configure list -- it shows the active region and where that setting is coming from.

If your CI/CD environment uses region variables, make sure both AWS_REGION and AWS_DEFAULT_REGION are set to the same value. Different tools read different variables, and a mismatch means some tools talk to one region while others talk to another.
After fixing a wrong-region deploy, go back and delete the accidental copy of the resource in the wrong region. Leftover resources cost money and confuse anyone who looks at the account later.
